Transcript:
So,
first I want to talk a little bit about
knowledge. So, one of the problems that
organizations run into at every size
when they start to use AI agents is
their data is not all in the same place
or at least it's not all in a way that
agents can properly use. So, most of the
time people get excited about AI, they
start to use it more, they realize how
important the context is, and then
they're like, "Oh, shoot. Our data isn't
where it needs to be for us to properly
be able to use this and then use AI
agents well." There's another challenge
that comes up
which is um
around shareability of skills. So, one
of the arguments I'm going to make and
if you kind of run a company or you work
at a company, um
you'll you've heard me talk about this
if you've come before, but
if you run a company or or you work at a
company,
there's
AI workflows that you want at a company
level.
Hey, here is a how's a how's a as a
company how we do X, right? So, like
let's say for me it's webinars, right? I
do uh this live class every single week.
There's a way that we do it. Hello Ben,
welcome back. Good to see you again.
Um
there's a way that I do these webinars.
And I want anyone to be able to follow
that. If I were to hire somebody to take
over these webinars for some reason, um
they would take over my process, right?
I want that process to be done every
time. And now let's say that person is
doing the webinar and uh they leave. I
don't want how we do webinars to leave
with that person. I want that to be a a
workflow. How we've tried to solve this
before in the past is we create SOPs,
but that requires a human to go and
follow the SOP and do the work as
outlined in the SOP, but the the output
of that work is going to be different
cuz that person is different. Like a
great um
way to think about this is if you give
five different directors the script to a
movie and tell them to go make a movie,
you get five different movies, same
script. Same thing happens with people.
Well, one one of the benefits of AI is
when you build out these workflows, you
get a lot more consistency in the
output. And then the person who's
executing the output, they're using
their judgment to make sure that things
kind of stay on the rails.
And there's a shareability problem here,
too. Like you want a set of skills that
AI can use to help your employees do
work, and you want to make sure that
employees all benefit from the
improvement of the skills that make AI
do the work better. Now, what a lot of
folks have is one person who uses AI
really well. They might build some
skills that help them do their job that
other people would benefit from, but
those aren't really shared, and those
people are using different skills. It's
kind of all over the place. If you want
to like unify your strategy, that's what
we're going to be talking about today,
in addition to some some new stuff that
I've been working on. If you're familiar
with loop engineering, it's great. But
what I want everyone to do quickly here,
if you could type into the chat, on a
scale from 1 to 10, 10 being like
incredibly sophisticated, and 1 being
like I I'm still just figuring out how
to use ChatGPT in the browser, like in
you know, on chat.openai.com in a
thread,
where would you put yourself on the AI
scale? Like how sophisticated are you as
a person, as an organization? Um you
know, let me give a good five. A five
would be like um I have a set of skills
that I use, and I repeat those skills,
but it's still like me going back and
forth, and I'm the one initiating those
skills, and I don't really have any
automation set up. But that'll be a
five. That's kind of in the middle. So,
just give me a number so I have a sense
here. And what I'm going to do is I'm
going to share with you
how um I recommend building out
the knowledge base, how that knowledge
base should get used, how we can share
some of that across the board, and I'll
also give you some examples. For some

how I like to have data all in one

that it can use. Now, I'll use the term
like in one place a lot. I don't mean
literally in one place. What I mean is
it's

relevant to each other, it probably
should be in one place. Um okay,

>> Let me show you an example
of
of having context all in one place.
All right. So, here we are. This is
my IG Reels performance. So, I create a

this data is really important to me.
It's what
what date I posted it, the caption,
here's a link to the post itself, uh how
many views it got, how many likes,
like rate, comments, you get the idea. A
whole bunch of this kind of this data
gets collected. And this is from the
Meta API, by the way. And we connect it
to the reel right from the Meta API. It
comes out, it's connected to a reel, and
we also transcribe that reel, so we have
the full text of the reel, and then I
also run it through a prompt that um
I created to do an analysis, a metrics
analysis of the reel. So, this is just
what the metrics seem to say about this
specific reel. We do this on every
single reel. And you can see some of my
videos here do terrible, right? Like uh
this one got 485 uh views, not good.
Some of them do awesome. This one got
3.3 million views. That's crazy. This
one got 100,000 views. This one got
17,000, right? So, it ranges, and we can
learn something from the successes. So,
level one of this is like, yeah, I just
put all of the reel data in one place.
That's great. If you're doing marketing
campaigns, you would put the marketing
performance all in one sheet. You might
need to connect that from different
places. Maybe part of the marketing
campaign was uh social media posts, and
some of it was emails, and you want to
put that all into one place.
Uh but then the next thing is AI
enrichment, right? And that's what I'm
doing here. I'm enriching this data with
a reels analysis that does what I used
to do manually to determine, "Hey, what
did I learn from the data of this reel
about this reel?" It does that based on
the performance data as well as the
transcript, okay?
If you're not following, feel free to
throw in questions. I'll answer them as
I go through. Okay. So, I get this all
into one place. This is a critical part
of what I'm about to show you. And I
enrich that data, right? So, we have our
database, which in this example is just
specific to the Instagram reel stuff,
and I enrich part of that data with AI.
Now, let's say you're doing like lead
lead stuff. So, we have inbound leads
come through, we want to enrich those
leads. Some of that enrichment can
happen just via web scraping or
different APIs that you want to use to
enrich that data. Other parts of that
data you might need AI to do. Maybe AI
needs to do an analysis of their website
in order to determine if this is a good
fit for your product or service. That
would be an example of an AI enriched
field. And maybe you're going to have
the LLM score that lead based on the
website and based on your best fits to
tell you like hey how similar is this to
a best fit
customer for you, okay?
That would be another example. So
there's a lot of ways you can do this.
The idea is we're going to putting it
all in one place, we're enriching what
we need to enrich with AI so that way we
have it as a workable surface that the
agents can access, okay? This is the key
part. Um this is where most folks are
lacking. Their CRM storing CRM stuff,
call recordings is in a different
software. Um
they listen to those call recordings and
give reps feedback in a completely
different place. Like everything is
disconnected.
Once you connect all those places, now
the AI can can do things with it, right?
Um
where you can run into a challenge,
let's go through my example there. You
might be collecting the call
transcripts, you might have the data in
the CRM, those are in two different
places. You can have the LLM go and look
those at those two different places,
right? Hey, find the deal in the CRM, go
find any transcripts in the thing.
These are extra steps and it creates it
can create problems. So I like getting
everything organized in one place. It
also allows you to be a little bit more
dynamic with how you use that data.

Different models have different
strengths, and you want to find the ones
that have a good set of strengths for
you. For me, the ability to do work for
me on my behalf is my number one
criteria. And if I ask Codex to
go into my marketing tool and do some
stuff, and it can't do it with the MCP
and the APIs maybe not working right or
it can't really do it with the API
either. Uh it will log into the browser
and it will do the thing. It won't stop
and say, "Oh, I can't do it like that."
Then I have to creatively come up with a
solution and then ask it to do it. Like
it exhausts all resources, and because
of that it's very valuable to me. Uh

this computer, which is my laptop, is
also connected to this. Wherever I'm at,
I can access that computer, and I'm I'm
helping her with some of her sales and
marketing stuff. She can do the same
thing and I can just kick off agents to

can log into the browser and do

here. Like Gemini's not really
um it doesn't really have this kind of

here I want to point out, cost. Cost is

your actual limits. When they first came

infinite usage and it felt incredible.
You could do anything like this is
amazing. Um well,
with um
if you look at how much you're spending
versus how much token usage you get,
nothing beats these two. And it's
because they they fight for users cuz
they need the users to train models
better. When you look at like Perplexity
computer, they're making a margin on
every token that they spend. So if you
look at Codex here versus Claude, like
uh ChatGPT on the $200 a month plan, you
get if I remember right, it's $14,000 of
tokens. For Claude, uh you get like
$8,000 of tokens. So you just get a lot
more for your dollar. So that's why I I
I chose those. Okay, I sort of tell the
time on that, but I just think it's
important. Okay, so as you're using
the agent,
what happens is you do tasks that create
some kind of memory, okay? And this

hey, Kyle doesn't like m-dashes. That's

have a set of skills that get used. It's
an email campaign drafting skill.

actually in a minute here, but um
campaign drafting skill. It's going to
be uh a proposal writing skill.
Mar- marketing like uh
promotional email skills. Like all of
these different skills that people might
use is going to be here, too. This for
Codex and Cloud co-work gets stored
locally.
on people's machines. And one of the
problems that happens is they have a
skill that's on their machine, it's in
their fol- folder for for Codex or
whatever they're using or the project
specifically. And that's where changes
to those skills happen. You want these
skills to get better over time, and
there's a couple of ways to do that. Uh
but but it's important that these skills
get better over time because as people
do work and they run into the model
outputting things that aren't that good,
they will give feedback to the model.
And they'll say, "Hey, rewrite this
email, but make it more like this."
Why did they do that?
Cuz they have a preference. They have
taste. They're making a decision. And
you want to capture the reasoning behind
that so you can improve the skill.
And then next time that skill gets used
either by that person or another person,
it makes it better for everybody, okay?
So,
that's what's happening while you're
using it, and you're storing that kind
of stuff. Like, hey, we got feedback
when using this skill that this sort of
thing sucked, right? And then what
happened Oh, there's supposed to be an
arrow here. Just make it real quick.
Here to here. Oh, jeez.
Okay.
>> [clears throat]
>> Um right here, what happens is we're
going to approve
memory as well as kind of skill updates.
That's what this is supposed to say. As
well as skill updates. So, right here,
we're going to get some feedback on
skills. We're also going to have
different memories that you might want
stored while you're doing work. The
agent can pull out memories on how it's
done work like this before in the past,
right? Things that aren't directly
related to the skill. And a human
approves
these skill updates and the memories.
And this is an important part. Like,
there's a few ways to do this. You can
do You can have an LLM responsible for
approving certain types of memories. And
for memories, you could probably get
away with that. For skill updates, I
really think the human needs to be
involved. Um
So, anyway,
the agent is responsible for collecting
these things. A human's responsible for
approving it. And if the human approves
a memory, it goes back into the memory
layer. If the human approves a skill
update, this is where that skill gets
updated. And usually, you'll you'll
store this in GitHub. It gets approved,
and then that skill update is now the
most updated skills, and there's a local
sync that keeps that skill synced with
the most recent version. And this is a
way to have some shareability in the
skills that you use. If not, all skills
will start drifting, and the question is
which one do you want to use? Now, you
can do it that way. I'm not saying you
shouldn't. But if one of the problems
you're running into is, "Hey, everyone's
using different stuff, and I want our
skills to be consistent across
everybody." And this is a way to do it.
Now, where I want to transition that to
is
something that I've been working with
called loops. So, some people have heard
of loops.
Um
This is if you've been building out um
kind of AI workflows for a long time,
we've been doing loop style things for a
while. Um or at least I have been and a
lot of people I know, we've been do
building these types of systems out.
Um
>> [clears throat]
>> there's a new term called loop
engineering and I want to talk
about it in the context of how we
improve a skill over time and I'll show
you a real example that I use. Um so,
right here you have a human prompt. So,
this might be like, "Hey, I want to
create a webinar." I'm going to show you
my webinar one because it um I have a
loop built for that. Um hey, I want to
create a webinar about X topic.
Well, what you used to do is used to say
that and then you might have a skill
built out for this, but you're going
back and forth with the LLM, giving it
feedback, it's coming up with ideas. Oh,
okay, yeah, I like this idea. Yeah, the
title should be this or maybe change the
learning bullets to this type of thing
here and you know, I don't like that
email. I think it should be more written
like this and you go back and forth as
the human. What you can do and what you
should be doing is you should be
building loops that do the back and
forth for you based on how you
want things to come out. When we have a
workflow, we also know what good looks
good looks like and what we can build
out is when that prompt comes through,
we run a loop, the AI outputs, let's say
a promo email for a webinar,
an AI evaluator evaluates that output
and gives it feedback, which gives it a
better AI output, the evaluator looks at
the new output, gives it more feedback,
and that process of loops, that's why
it's called a loop, until you get the
best possible output.
And then your back and forth happens a
lot less frequently. You're not going
back and forth on every little thing.
And when you do, so let's say you run
this loop and you do,
at the very end of this loop, once the
human gives the final feedback,
it improves the skills or whatever it
needs to improve, the skills, the
evaluation, um the example set. It
improves all of those things to make the
next time that skill gets used even
better, okay? So, let me show you a real
example. It doesn't seem like anyone's
using loops today. So, I'm going to show
you um a real example of this. I'm going
to start a new thread.
Um
open this.
And let me change what screen I'm
sharing.
Also, can I get some feedback while I'm
I'm sharing this? Is this helpful for
folks? Just to give me a yes if this is
like it this is helpful for you, you're
understanding, we're on the right track
here. If there's any questions, you can
feel free to throw them in there. Okay,
this is a Codex. This is my uh kind of
like my main folder that I work out of
and do all the stuff I want to do. All
right. So, what we're going to do is
we're going to create a webinar. I'm
going to show you how I I personally
create these webinars, okay? This is how
I do it. And And mostly I'm creating all
of the uh material for that webinar. So,
what I'll do um let's do what what kind
of webinar should we create? Uh let's do
one on
maybe designing and building loops.
Let's just Let's just do uh I don't
know. That's That one's just like
basically what we're talking about now.
Um [clears throat]
let's do a webinar. If you have an idea,
definitely throw it in there. Let's do a
webinar on
let's do a webinar on how to get um your
employees actually use AI.
I want to use the webinar
loop to create a webinar about how
employees
how you can get your employees to use AI
more. Um I think the main benefit of
that is that um
your employees will be able to do a lot
more and you'll be a lot more effective
with that one person.
Um, but sometimes it's like everyone's
at a different level with their AI
usage. Some people are still just using
a chat thread, some people are more
comfortable using the agent, some people
aren't, some people don't fully
understand how it works, they don't
really trust like where the problems
might be when using AI. There's a lot of
friction points and because of that's
what ends up happening is you have some
employees that use AI really well and
then you have other employees that don't
use it very much or they just kind of
use it every now and then and there
becomes this weird thing where the
employees who aren't using it that much
don't really get like how powerful can
be and they haven't had that aha moment
of like, oh, how I work has completely
changed. And then the employees who have
and their their their work has
completely changed
and that's where like the spotlight goes
and then they get all the credit um, and
you want all of your employees to get a
lot of value out of the AI tools that
you're using
because there's a bunch of different
benefits of it. All right, there we go.
That was like kind of rambling on but
you get the idea. Okay, so all I'm doing
is I'm I just did a talk to text of of
everything that I wanted.
And I have already saved this loop. Now,
what kind of technically a loop is it's
a script that kind of runs the loop for
you. So, I have a prompt that builds a
loop if you want to you can just comment
it below and I and if somebody says they
want I'll send it to you but if you're
not if you if you're not ready for this
stuff I I don't need to send it to you.
Uh, but anyway, it builds the loop cuz
the loop is a script and it's and it's
stored on my local machine here. So,
what's going to happen here is it's
going to grab the loop and kick off the
loop process. Part of that loop is using
the webinar creation skill for
generating a brief. There's also one for
creating, um,

we have webinar content orchestrator.

>> Okay, [clears throat] so what's
happening now is it's like getting the
the loop ready. Also, I have something
set up with my Codex. Every single time
I kick off something, it saves it as a
task in a task manager that I like.
This is specifically for me. I don't
like using task managers. I don't use
them. I never check them when I have
them. I'm just not that guy. So what I
do is I have AI kind of manage the task.
So if I ever need to look back and say
like, "Hey, what did we do?" or "Did we
do this thing?" it can go and like check
it. I don't actually look at it and
it'll mark them complete and does
everything for me. So it kind of like
does an auto trail of all the things
that I work on and then when I forget
stuff, it'll be like, "Hey, by the way,
you started working on this, you didn't
finish it." Okay, so right here it's

this? I don't care about that. I've

sections. So that bothers me and I can
tell it to to make a change to that. Why
am I bringing this up? I'm bringing this
up because one of the things I think is
really powerful about using AI tools and
using agents to do work.
Um there's a couple. Like one of the big
ones is you can just do a whole bunch of
things at the same time, which is sweet.
Like as you know like it's going into
beehive and checking into the problem
that you guys had opting in for the the
loop building prompt.
Um so like that's great. But the other
thing is it can be morphed around how
you like to work. So for example, I
built a loop with a buddy of mine
um to write blog posts and articles and
stuff. He does a lot of content
marketing and that's part of his job. Uh
so him and I built it together based on
his stuff, right? Not really me. I was I
was more just the loop guy. Uh we built
out this loop together and I got
feedback from him and I implemented that
feedback try to refine the loop more. I
I give him the loop. Okay, it's his loop
now. I still have my own my own loop. He
will continue giving feedback. He writes
more articles than I do. He will keep
giving feedback. His loop is going to
get stronger and stronger and stronger
over time because he's going to keep
giving it feedback.
My loop is going to be kind of fixed
until I create a blog post. Then when
that blog post happens, I'm going to
give feedback. My loop is going to get
better and better for my lens. Now let's
fast forward 6 months. Both of us have
been working independently. Let's say
we're both creating a blog posts.
We have two very different styles of
blog posts that we like. The loop has
been morphed to fit what we each like.
The blog posts that come out are going
to be very different and he might not
like some of the stuff that I like.
That's what's I think is so powerful
about this stuff. It's it can be bent
and morphed into how people like. It's
also an a counter argument to having
everything very shareable by the way. Um
one of the things I think is going to
happen is I think what we're going to
see is we're going to see a lot less
employees per company. And when we have
less employees per company, it means
that you're going to have one person
who's has a lot of responsibilities and
builds out and a work environment
that serves them very well, and it's a
portable work environment. Like I can
use this to create a webinar for every
anything, not just me.

The key
thing I want to highlight is the quality
of this output will be as good
as you have taught it to be over time.
And this is where we start getting to a
different world. Like back when I first
started doing AI stuff, the prompt
engineering was so critical, which it
still is. But um this is probably like
the least important skill now. Like
context, like how you manage context way
more important than the prompts. Um
so like prompt engineering was all you
cared about. You tried to write the best
prompts possible to get as close as a to
a one shot, right? Like hey, I send this
prompt and it one shots like with one
prompt writes the perfect thing. And
then
systems got a lot better at
understanding what you were asking, and
it became the context you're giving it
was the bottom line. Where if you gave
it too much context, it would get all
weird. Um you know, threads, like it
didn't manage your thread very well. So
if like your thread went too long,
everything would go off the rails,
right? Context engineering became really
important, and then it's still
important. Um there's a self-learning
element here. This is what I want to
call out. A self-learning element here
that is really important because it's
learning from your actual work and where
you put most of your attention is in the
feedback you give on how to improve
these skills. So, right here um

this would be like a more aligned with

Um this is this is a part and then I'm
I'm giving you this as a demonstration
here, but this is a part of what I think
we're going to start seeing a lot more
of. And eventually, this is just going
to be built into all of these tools. For
now, like I've built each one of these I
used that prompt that I just shared with
you guys in order to do it. Um
eventually, they'll probably just get
more built in that way
where your skills are self-learning.
And the thing that I want you to keep
with you is with self-learning skills,
you as a human should still be there to
make sure that the learnings and match
what you actually want the changes to be
reflected of.
Because AI goes off the rails often. It
wants to do all these crazy changes when
like one sentence of a prompt matters,
right? And that's probably where I'm
going to close this thing out uh cuz I
think it's a good feedback for uh for
everybody here.
Um
Often when I'm asked to make changes, so
like right after this, I'm probably
going to play around and and kind of fix
this loop
a bit. Um so, that's what I'm going to
do.
It's going to want to make some pretty
aggressive changes. It's like, "Oh, he
didn't like that first sentence. So, we
should probably change this whole
section of the prompt which had this
kind of examples. We should do this kind
of thing here and this kind of thing
here." It's going to make all the like
want to make all these changes. It's
like, "Eh."
I think you should just make one change
to the skill.
And then change would probably be this.
And it's because I have a lot of
experience prompt engineering that I can
give that kind of feedback. And my
encouragement to everybody is to be
really thoughtful
about what changes you want to make to
skills that you're building and you're
actively working on. So then that way
they get better over time because of
your guidance.
If you don't put your guidance in there,
what happens is these big changes happen
and things go completely off the rails.

changes are just silly. Like I read
them, I'm like, this is not This is way
overkill. Um like oh oh I'll give you a
real example. Um the subject lines in

this um this week. The subject lines

when Another thing to throw on your

there's certain like the agents.md file
gets loaded every turn of the

other files that get grabbed um when

files in skills. So, for example, every

kicks on, looks at the entire thread's

on the on the uh
on the computer.

look at all the feedback Kyle gave, and
then go boom, here's my my suggested.

cuz it'll make suggestions, and then I
have emails that go, "Ah, that's a bad
suggestion. This is might be a good one.

keeps re-running. And then I get the
final suggestions, and then I make
feedback, and then of course, at the end
of that loop, we run a loop to improve
balance as well.
